cycles.name: Foundation Forge
focuses: Build foundational movement quality and neuromuscular efficiency across all major patterns.
Establish aerobic base and running consistency toward half marathon preparation.

cycles.name: Strength Engine
focuses: Build relative strength through progressive overload with new tools and increased training volume.
Extend Zone 2 run duration and consolidate walk-run protocol toward continuous running.

cycles.name: Metabolic Shift
focuses: Drive body recomposition through higher training density and elevated metabolic demand.
Develop single-leg and running-specific strength for race durability.

paragraphs: You went quiet for almost a month, and then you came back — lighter, honest about it, and you've shown up every session since. That's the whole game, Pegah. Cycle 3 is built on that: three focused days, nothing wasted, everything earns its place.

This is the cycle where the body composition work gets real. The proof is already there — your leg press doubled from 20 to 41kg in Cycle 2 — so now we make the sessions denser, put real load on your glutes with the hip thrust machine you like, and turn Day 2 into one clean job: run. Your mental focus this block is simple — protect the rhythm. Three days, every week, at whatever intensity that day allows.
By August 16 you'll be visibly stronger on every lift, running longer continuous stretches inside your zone, and your calves and glutes will be carrying you like a runner's should.
outcomes: Machine hip thrust loaded and progressing week over week
25–30 minutes of continuous running inside 139–152 bpm
First cycle of direct calf training complete — race-ready lower legs started
All three mandatory days hit, every week of the cycle
Every lift logged with honest RPE — including the soreness score

cycles.name: Race Ready
focuses: Peak running volume and muscular endurance for half marathon completion.
Maintain lean muscle and manage fatigue through smart load programming.

paragraphs: Everything until now has been construction. Cycle 4 is where you stop training to become a runner and start training like one — the race stops being an idea on a calendar and becomes the thing you're measurably ready for.
The strength you're building right now is exactly what will hold your form together in the final kilometres.
Five weeks. Earn the start line.

cycles.name: Consolidate & Elevate
focuses: Integrate all physical qualities into a sustainable, high-performance lifestyle structure.
Post-race recovery, reflection, and transition into long-term athletic identity.

days.completionMessage: The heaviest day of your week is behind you — legs loaded, finisher survived, engine fed. Eat properly tonight; Day 2's run will thank you for it.

good: Conversational pace — this is just raising body temperature
Feel the legs waking up while the breathing stays easy
bad: Turning it into cardio — this is a warm-up, not a workout

good: Push the knees out against the band — hips rise to a straight line from knee to shoulder
Feel the glutes fire harder against the band's pull
bad: Letting the band collapse the knees inward — active resistance is the goal

good: Opposite arm and leg reach long — body stays parallel to the floor
Feel the core bracing to stop the hips rotating
bad: Arching the lower back as the leg extends — reduce the range if it happens

good: Pull the band to the chest with long arms
Feel the shoulder blades squeezing together
bad: Shrugging the shoulders toward the ears

good: Feet hip-width on the platform, knees track over toes through the full range
Feel the quads and glutes loading equally — drive through the whole foot
bad: Locking the knees fully at the top — keep a slight bend to hold tension

good: Hinge over the pad and rise until the body forms one straight line — no higher
Feel the glutes and hamstrings pulling you up — squeeze the glutes hard at the top
bad: Hyperextending past straight at the top or rounding through the spine

good: Handles press in a smooth arc — chest proud, shoulders back and down
Feel the chest doing the pressing — squeeze the elbows toward each other
bad: Letting the shoulders roll forward at lockout

good: Bar pulls to the upper chest — elbows drive down and back
Feel the lats pulling the elbows toward the hips
bad: Leaning back or swinging to generate momentum

good: Arms lift in a smooth arc to shoulder height with a slight elbow bend
Feel the side delts doing the lifting, not the traps
bad: Shrugging or swinging the weights up

good: Body stays rigid — the only thing moving is the tapping arm
Feel the obliques firing to stop the hips rotating
bad: Hips twisting or shifting toward the raised arm

good: A sharp hip snap floats the bell to shoulder height — arms are just ropes
Feel the glutes loading on the backswing and firing at the top
bad: Squatting the movement — the knees must not travel forward

good: Squat deep, drive up, and release the ball to the wall target
Feel the legs and hips generating the throw — the arms only guide
bad: Throwing with the arms only — if the legs are not driving, the pattern is wrong

days.completionMessage: Every minute inside that zone is aerobic engine you keep forever. One job today — done.

good: Jog at a pace that allows full sentences — walk the moment your heart rate passes 152
Keep the breathing rhythmic and controlled — the goal is 25–30 continuous minutes by Week 5
bad: Pushing through above-zone effort — walking to recover IS the protocol, not a failure

days.completionMessage: Glutes, single-leg strength, calves — everything that holds a half marathon together got stronger today. That's the session future race-day you is most grateful for.

good: Conversational pace — this is just raising body temperature
Feel the legs waking up while the breathing stays easy
bad: Turning it into cardio — this is a warm-up, not a workout

good: Lunge position — hand inside the front foot, top arm reaches toward the ceiling
Feel the thoracic spine opening and the back-leg hip flexor stretching
bad: Rushing — each rep should be a genuine new position, not a fast transition

good: Tall torso, hips gently driven forward
Feel the stretch through the front of the back hip — breathe into it on each exhale
bad: Arching the lower back to gain more range — the movement comes from the hip

good: Stay low, steps wide, band taut the whole way
Feel the outer glutes working as they push the knees out
bad: Standing tall or letting the knees dive inward

good: Long stance, both feet planted — drop the back knee straight down toward the floor
Feel the front glute and quad pushing the floor away — weight stays on the front foot
bad: Front knee collapsing inward or the torso pitching forward

good: Drive the hips up to a flat tabletop against the pad
Feel the glutes contract hard at the top — a full squeeze before lowering
bad: Hyperextending the lower back to finish the rep

exercises.name: Half Kneeling Single Arm Cable Row

good: Elbow drives in a straight line back to the hip — body stays completely still
Feel the lat pulling the elbow, not the bicep curling
bad: Rotating the torso to complete the rep — if rotating, reduce the weight

good: Handles press straight up — rib cage stays down throughout
Feel both shoulders pressing equally — the machine guides the path, focus purely on contraction
bad: Arching the lower back off the seat — brace before every set

good: Hips stay pressed into the pad — only the lower leg moves
Feel the hamstrings curling the weight, not the glutes compensating
bad: Letting the hips rise off the pad to finish the rep

good: Rise all the way onto the balls of the feet, pause, then lower slowly to a full stretch
Feel the calves stretch fully at the bottom and squeeze hard at the top
bad: Bouncing out of the bottom — the slow lowering is the entire point

good: Pull the rope to the face with the elbows high and wide
Feel the rear delts and upper back pulling — the hands just hold on
bad: Turning it into a row — the elbows stay at shoulder height

good: Opposite arm and leg lower slowly toward the floor
Feel the lower back staying glued to the floor for the whole rep
bad: The back arching off the floor — shorten the range if it does

good: Lower back pressed flat, arms overhead, legs long
Feel the whole anterior core holding the shape
bad: The lower back lifting away from the floor — bend the knees to regress

days.completionMessage: Nobody required this one — you chose it. That's the habit that carries you to the start line.

good: A pace that feels almost too easy — fully sustainable the entire time
Feel the body moving and recovering — not working or pushing
bad: Letting this turn into a workout — the moment it feels like effort, slow down

cards.title: The Comeback Was the Win
cards.body: Twenty-five days off, and you came back anyway — lighter, shorter, honest about not being 100%. That's exactly how it's done. Understand what this cycle is protecting: not perfection, rhythm. Three days a week, every week, beats five days one week and zero the next — the gap taught us both that. If a hard day comes, the protocol is drop every RPE by 1 and show up anyway. Showing up modified counts as a full win.

cards.title: Your Leg Press Doubled — Keep Pulling
cards.body: 20kg to 41kg in one interrupted cycle — that's the fastest-moving lift in your program and the proof your body responds when you load it. The rule for this cycle: when all 4 sets of 10 feel like RPE 7 or easier, add 2.5kg the next session. Don't wait for me to tell you each week — the trigger is yours now. Same logic applies everywhere: chest press +2kg, lat pulldown +2–3kg, when the RPE says so.

cards.title: Hip Thrust Machine — Your New Main Lift
cards.body: You told me you like the hip thrust machine, so it's now a primary — 4 sets of 10, with a full one-second squeeze at the top of every rep. This is the single most important lift in the program for both goals: glute strength drives your running stride and your body composition. Week 1, find a weight that's a true RPE 7 and own the squeeze. From Week 2, add 5kg whenever all four sets feel controlled.

cards.title: Split Squat — Both Feet Down
cards.body: The split squat replaces the reverse lunge: same single-leg strength, but both feet stay planted, so all your focus goes into the working leg instead of balancing. Long stance, back knee drops straight down, weight through the front foot. This is your race-durability lift — half marathons are run one leg at a time. Start with the 4kg dumbbells you used for lunges and build from there.

cards.title: Day 2 Is Just the Run Now
cards.body: No circuit, no core finisher — you were running out of time and cutting them anyway, so I cut them for you. Day 2 has exactly one job: 35–40 minutes inside 139–152 bpm. Walk the moment your heart rate passes 152 — that's the protocol working, not you failing. The goal by Week 5 is 25–30 minutes of continuous running inside the zone. The conditioning you lost didn't disappear; it moved to a 7-minute finisher on Day 1.

cards.title: Calves — The Half Marathon Insurance
cards.body: New this cycle: standing calf raises, and they matter more than they look. Your calves and Achilles absorb more force per stride than any other tissue in distance running, and until now nothing in your program loaded them directly. The slow 3-second lowering into a full stretch is the entire point — never bounce out of the bottom. Fifteen quality reps at RPE 7. Your future race-day legs are built here.

cards.title: Log the Real Numbers
cards.body: Your July 8 session dipped for one clear reason: 2-out-of-5 sleep the night before — and the readiness check caught it, which is exactly why it exists. Keep filling it honestly. One thing I need from you: the soreness score has read "none" on every single entry since June. If that's true, great — but if you've been skipping past it, start rating it for real. I set your loads from these numbers; honest data gets you a better program.
